text:
Doc intent
This doc estimates the expected error-budget impact of shipping “workload-aware batching defaults” (fast / standard / cost-efficient latency SLO tiers), and defines SRE guardrails + alert routing for the staged rollout.

Audience: SRE on-call, Runtime on-call, Observability, Release Engineering, Hosted API PM rollout owners.
Owner: SRE (Sean). Runtime implementation owner: Sofia/Lena team.

TL;DR
- If we keep fast-tier tail protections strict and maintain overload backpressure behavior, the change should be neutral-to-positive on error budget for standard traffic (better queue stability due to consistent defaults, fewer customer misconfigs).
- Primary error-budget risks are (1) fast-tier p99 regressions under mixed workloads, (2) queue blowups in cost-efficient tier during bursty concurrency, and (3) telemetry/label cardinality incidents masking real performance signals (al...

text:
Purpose
- Ensure SRE/oncall is ready for the limited production rollout of “Quality guardrails gating for Optimize” (hosted API subset of routes/accounts).
- This checklist is specifically for: new alerts, dashboards, runbooks, and rehearsals.
- Target window: limited prod rollout starting ~2026-02; 24/7 oncall coverage expected.

Rollout summary (what’s new for oncall)
- Optimize recommendations (batching, caching, quant profile changes) are now blocked unless quality gates pass.
- Gate runs depend on eval-harness GPU capacity + artifact storage + Optimize service orchestration.
- Rollout controller will halt/pause canary promotion if gates fail (defense-in-depth: enforcement in Optimize apply boundary + rollout promotion).
- New failure modes:
 - “False positive” gate failures due to flakiness or variance
 - Gate run timeouts / queue backlog (especially regional GPU scarcity)
 - Gat...

Scope / constraints

Quick glossary (as used in aler...
text:
Context
- Canary: runtime-1.22 KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction behind feature flag.
- Goal of this doc: record what was noisy during canary, what we changed, and why (so we don’t re-learn this next rollout).
- Owners: SRE (Sean/Allison/Rafael) w/ runtime input (Omar/Samir).

Quick glossary (as used in alerts/dashboards)
- frag_ratio: fragmentation ratio in KV arena (0–1). Higher = more fragmented.
- compaction_bytes_moved: bytes moved due to compaction (rate + total).
- compaction_step_ms: time spent in compaction “step” (budgeted).
- eviction_events: sequences/prefixes evicted (tier-tagged).
- decode_p99_ms: p99 decode latency (route/model/tier).

What we learned (high-level)
1) The first wave of alerting tied compaction activity too directly to paging/latency spikes.
 - Compaction activity ≠ bad. Some compaction is expected and healthy, especially immediately after enablin...

1) Purpose

text:
Owner: Sean Gallagher (SRE)
Last reviewed with: Aisha Bello (dashboards/alerts), Kevin Osei (exporter), Logan Wright (coverage telemetry)
Status: draft (targeting “SLO v1” in same release window as schema v1 rollout)

---

1) Purpose
We are standardizing audit log event schema + expanding coverage for sensitive actions across Hosted/Dedicated/Private. This doc proposes:
- SLIs/SLOs for the audit log pipeline (delivery latency + drop rate)
- alert thresholds (paging vs ticket) and burn-rate policies
- an error budget policy appropriate for enterprise security expectations

Scope in this doc: the platform’s ability to accept, durably ingest, and make audit events available for downstream export/consumption.
Out of scope: correctness of event contents/taxonomy (handled by schema + coverage workstreams), and customer SIEM availability/ingestion beyond our exporter boundary.

---

title: Shadow Traffic: Failure Mode Review (SRE) — scoring/telemetry/shadow-target degradation
preview:
Context
- Shadow traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow targets” (candidate model/version/quant profile/prompt) while returning the control response to the user.
- Core reliability promise: shadowing MUST NOT impact the user path (latency, availability, correctness of contr...
text:
Context
- Shadow traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow targets” (candidate model/version/quant profile/prompt) while returning the control response to the user.
- Core reliability promise: shadowing MUST NOT impact the user path (latency, availability, correctness of control response). Shadow path is fail-open by design.
- This review focuses on: (1) scoring pipeline down / lagging, (2) telemetry ingestion lag/drops, (3) shadow target errors/overload/timeouts, plus cross-cutting risks (cardinality, quotas, backpressure).

Definitions (for shared language)
- Control path: the request that serves the user response.
- Shadow mirror: async/in-parallel invocation to shadow target(s) with strict timeout budget and isolation.
- Shadow artifact: request/response pair metadata emitted to telemetry, plus optional redacted prompt/response payload refs....

title: Rollback Gameday Plan — Optimization Config Safe Apply + One-Click Rollback
preview:
Doc intent
- This is the run-of-show for a 90-minute gameday drill validating: detection, pause, rollback, and verification for a bad Optimization Config rollout (batching/caching/quant profiles).
- This doc is written for SRE/on-call + platform/runtime participants. If something is unclear, update this doc during/a...
text:
Doc intent
- This is the run-of-show for a 90-minute gameday drill validating: detection, pause, rollback, and verification for a bad Optimization Config rollout (batching/caching/quant profiles).
- This doc is written for SRE/on-call + platform/runtime participants. If something is unclear, update this doc during/after.

Why we’re doing this
- We’ve had real incidents/regressions from optimization changes (mis-scoped applies, latency spikes, partial rollout states). Gameday should prove we can:
 - Detect regressions quickly via the Rollout Health dashboard + alerts
 - Use the Safe Apply gates appropriately (pause/resume/abort)
 - Execute one-click rollback (Console + API) and return to baseline safely
 - Confirm audit events + tags are emitted (tenant/model/region/config_version)
 - Measure time-to-mitigate (TTM) and find friction points

Success criteria (what “good” looks like)
- D...

text:
Doc purpose
- Ensure we have enough benchmark fleet capacity (GPU-hours + scheduling windows + isolation) to produce the Q1 2026 “golden baseline” and stand up ongoing perf-canary/gating.
- This is an internal planning sheet; numbers are directional and should be revalidated after the first full sweep (week of Jan 13).

Scope covered by this plan
- Regions: us-east (primary), eu-west (secondary)
- SKUs: H100 80GB, A100 80GB, L40S 48GB (where applicable)
- Workloads (representative suite v1):
 1) Chat streaming (short prompts, variable decode, streaming)
 2) Long-context summarization / prefill stress (large prefill, modest decode)
 3) Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn; modeled as bursty chat with forced pauses)
 4) Embeddings high-QPS (small payload, throughput + tail latency)
- Models: “top deployed” set per baseline plan (Llama-family, Qwen-family, Mistral-family variants). E...

Status: DRAFT. Numbers below are intentionally conservative with headroom; we should validate against smoke-test harness + 2 reference clusters (VPC + on-prem-ish) before copying into installer defaults.

Scope of this doc

- What we do NOT size here: the customer’s SIEM/log backend (Splunk/ELK/etc), long-term metrics storage, multi-cluster federation, or “premium” observability.

Assumptions (v1 minimum pack)
- Kubernetes cluster running Redwood Private control plane + data plane (serving runtime pods).
- Prometheus-based metrics collection (Prometheus Operator / kube-prometheus-stack style deployment).

title: Burst neighbor impact analysis (tail latency + detection signals + circuit breaker thresholds) — v0.3
preview:
Context
- Dedicated “burst capacity” lets a tenant exceed their reserved baseline by borrowing GPUs from a shared, Redwood-managed burst pool (per region + hardware class).
- Primary SRE concern: bursting can create noisy-neighbor effects within a pool or upstream shared components (router, tokenization, kv-cache in...
text:
Context
- Dedicated “burst capacity” lets a tenant exceed their reserved baseline by borrowing GPUs from a shared, Redwood-managed burst pool (per region + hardware class).
- Primary SRE concern: bursting can create noisy-neighbor effects within a pool or upstream shared components (router, tokenization, kv-cache infra) that degrade tail latency (p95/p99) and/or error rates for non-burst tenants.
- This doc is analysis notes + initial thresholds. Expect revisions after load testing + beta telemetry.

Definitions (working)
- Baseline capacity: tenant’s reserved Dedicated allocation.
- Burst usage: traffic/compute above baseline admitted to the shared burst pool.
- Neighbor tenant: another Dedicated tenant in the same region/hardware class whose requests are NOT bursting (or bursting at a much lower level).
- Protected SLO (non-burst): We must not materially regress non-burst p95/p99 en...

title: Route Experiments (A/B Testing) — SRE On-call Prep Notes
preview:
Purpose
These notes are for SRE on-call readiness for Route-level Experiments ("route experiments" / A/B testing at the request-routing layer) ahead of broader beta expansion and GA. The primary SRE goals are:
- Detect and contain customer-visible impact quickly (wrong routing, elevated error/latency, runaway cost)....
text:
Purpose
These notes are for SRE on-call readiness for Route-level Experiments ("route experiments" / A/B testing at the request-routing layer) ahead of broader beta expansion and GA. The primary SRE goals are:
- Detect and contain customer-visible impact quickly (wrong routing, elevated error/latency, runaway cost).
- Ensure guardrails/auto-stop mechanisms are safe (avoid false positives causing churned traffic or incident loops).
- Provide a clear triage flow, escalation paths, and the minimum debugging evidence to collect.

Scope
- Control plane: experiment config API availability + config propagation (staleness) to routing layer
- Data plane: deterministic assignment + precedence with rollouts/fallback policies
- Telemetry: attribution integrity and metric cardinality safety
- Console: results freshness and query load (less critical than routing correctness, but can page if it thre...

text:
Owner(s)
- Policy owner: Sean Gallagher (SLO semantics / error budget policy)
- Oncall owner: Rafael Mendes (SRE oncall policy + paging)
- Implementation: Aisha Bello (alerting + dashboards), Sergio Costa (dashboards)
- Release lead escalation: Connor O’Brien (change freeze enforcement)

Status
- Draft for internal preview. Target: align ahead of Dedicated tier internal enablement + limited GA.

1) Purpose
We are introducing a Dedicated throughput SLO with tiered commitments. This policy defines:
- how we interpret throughput error budget consumption
- when error budget status gates production changes (routing/runtime/infrastructure)
- when we escalate to an incident (and who gets paged)
- how we coordinate with Product/CS on customer comms when a Dedicated deployment is at risk

This is intended to prevent “death by a thousand cuts” where we keep shipping while the throughput floor i...

text:
Owner: Infrastructure/SRE (Sean)
Last sanity check: 2025-03-01

Purpose
- Single place to map:
 (1) cloud regions (public IDs) -> Redwood internal region IDs
 (2) residency “zones” used in policy config + routing
 (3) default failover groups used by smart routing
 (4) which platform services are allowed per residency tier

Important notes (please read)
- Internal region IDs are stable identifiers used in routing logs, residency decision context, and policy objects.
- “Residency zone” is the customer-facing grouping used for allowlists (e.g., EU, US, APAC). For v1 we keep zones coarse, and the actual enforcement is at *region* granularity.
- Failover behavior for pinned traffic is constrained by ADR-021:
 - Cross-region fallback is disabled by default.
 - Fallback is only allowed to *other allowed regions* in the policy.
- Any service that handles “customer content” MUST be region-loca...

text:
Context / why this doc exists
- We need a repeatable, supportable way to prove that a given Redwood Private deployment can be restored from backup reliably.
- This is NOT just “restore completed successfully” — it’s deterministic state correctness + a set of signals SRE/Support can use to confidently sign off.
- This plan is used for:
 (1) engineering validation during development
 (2) periodic restore drills in staging
 (3) customer acceptance / Solutions-led validation during onboarding for enterprise Private accounts

title: Dedicated Throughput SLO – metric exploration notes (SRE scratch)
preview:
Context
- Dedicated is moving toward formal throughput guarantees (tiers + shapes). We need a canonical metric that is: (a) hard to game, (b) stable across model/version + routing changes, (c) aligns with how customers experience “throughput”, and (d) can be enforced/alerted with reasonable operational burden.
- Thi...
text:
Context
- Dedicated is moving toward formal throughput guarantees (tiers + shapes). We need a canonical metric that is: (a) hard to game, (b) stable across model/version + routing changes, (c) aligns with how customers experience “throughput”, and (d) can be enforced/alerted with reasonable operational burden.
- This doc captures SRE exploration (not final). Expect contradictions and open questions.

Goals for the metric
- Customer-relevant: reflects “how much work can I get done” on a Dedicated deployment.
- Comparable over time: changes in batching, caching, kernel selection, or model upgrades shouldn’t break continuity unless we explicitly change the commitment.
- Actionable: when we miss, we can quickly determine if it’s Redwood capacity, model regression, customer request pattern, or measurement artifact.
- Supports tier semantics: different floors, burst behavior, and possibly f...

text:
Purpose
- This is the SRE checklist + sign-off template for releasing a Runtime LTS patch (e.g., 1.18.1-lts) across Hosted/Dedicated/Private.
- Goal: prevent avoidable customer-impacting regressions by verifying (1) monitoring coverage, (2) rollback readiness, (3) load/perf results, (4) customer impact + comms.

When to run this review
- Required for every LTS patch release (scheduled monthly patches and out-of-band security fixes).
- Start: when RC build is cut and perf-canary gates are green (or waiver requested).
- End: prior to publishing artifacts / rollout start.

How to use
- Fill in the “Release metadata” and “Change summary” from the release PR.
- Attach links to perf reports, build artifacts, dashboards, and runbooks.
- Mark each item as PASS / FAIL / N/A and add notes.
- SRE sign-off is required unless explicitly waived by SRE on-call manager (rare; document waiver + ration...
